content.content:
1вҶ’# Local-Roots Product Strategy
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’## 1. Current Product Audit
     4вҶ’
     5вҶ’### What Is Built
     6вҶ’
     7вҶ’Local-Roots is a local pickup marketplace with a functional end-to-end flow for recurring farm box subscriptions.
     8вҶ’
     9вҶ’**Seller side (strong):**
    10вҶ’- Registration and authentication (JWT-based)
    11вҶ’- Store creation (one store per seller)
    12вҶ’- Pickup location setup with Google Places autocomplete and timezone handling
    13вҶ’- Subscription plan creation: title, cadence (weekly/biweekly/monthly), price, subscriber cap, first pickup date
    14вҶ’- Cycle generation: creates pickup windows, offerings, and orders for active subscribers automatically
    15вҶ’- Order fulfillment dashboard: mark ready, confirm pickup with 6-digit code (manual entry or QR scan), no-show handling with configurable fee
    16вҶ’- Farmstand QR code generation and printable poster for in-person marketing
    17вҶ’- Pause/resume box plans
    18вҶ’- Payout summary per pickup window (estimated seller payout, platform fee, no-show fees)
    19вҶ’- Store settings page
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’**Buyer side (functional):**
    22вҶ’- Browse stores, view boxes, subscribe via Stripe PaymentElement
    23вҶ’- Subscription management: pause, resume, cancel, update payment card
    24вҶ’- Order page with pickup code display and QR code for seller scan
    25вҶ’- Post-pickup reviews (1-5 rating + comment, unlocked after pickup confirmation)
    26вҶ’- Token-based access (no buyer accounts -- frictionless but limited)
    27вҶ’
    28вҶ’**Payments (partially complete):**
    29вҶ’- Stripe SetupIntent for future pickups (>7 days out), PaymentIntent with manual capture for near-term pickups
    30вҶ’- Webhook-driven payment status synchronization
    31вҶ’- Capture on pickup confirmation, partial capture for no-show fees
    32вҶ’- 5% buyer service fee
    33вҶ’- One-time orders exist at pay-at-pickup only (no card checkout for one-time)
    34вҶ’
    35вҶ’**Infrastructure:**
    36вҶ’- Next.js (App Router) frontend on Vercel
    37вҶ’- Go API backend on Railway with PostgreSQL
    38вҶ’- Well-structured schema: 12 migrations, proper constraints, composite foreign keys, concurrency-safe inventory reservation
    39вҶ’
    40вҶ’### Strengths
    41вҶ’
    42вҶ’1. **Subscription-first architecture.** The entire data model -- subscription plans, cycles, recurring orders -- is purpose-built for the recurring box use case. This is the right wedge; most competitors bolt subscriptions onto generic e-commerce.
    43вҶ’
    44вҶ’2. **Low seller setup friction.** The guided setup flow (location -> box -> review -> go live) gets a farmer from registration to accepting subscribers in minutes. Google Places autocomplete eliminates manual address entry.
    45вҶ’
    46вҶ’3. **Farmstand QR bridge.** This is a genuinely clever acquisition channel. A farmer prints a QR poster, tapes it to their booth, and walk-up market customers become recurring subscribers. This converts a one-time impulse buy into a subscription relationship -- the exact behavior that makes the unit economics work.
    47вҶ’
    48вҶ’4. **Fulfillment completeness.** The ready -> pickup code -> confirm flow with QR scanning is operationally sound. No-show handling with fee/waive options shows mature thinking about edge cases.
    49вҶ’
    50вҶ’5. **Payment architecture.** The SetupIntent / PaymentIntent split with manual capture is correct for this domain. Authorize early, capture on fulfillment. This protects both sides.
    51вҶ’
    52вҶ’6. **Schema quality.** Composite foreign keys, concurrency guards on inventory, proper constraint checks -- this will scale without data integrity issues.
    53вҶ’
    54вҶ’### Weaknesses
    55вҶ’
    56вҶ’1. **No seller payouts.** Stripe Connect is not integrated. Sellers cannot actually receive money. This is the single biggest blocker to real-world usage. A farmer will not use the platform if money stays in the platform's Stripe account indefinitely.
    57вҶ’
    58вҶ’2. **No buyer accounts.** Buyers access subscriptions and orders via token-based URLs. There is no login, no dashboard, no way to see all subscriptions in one place. This works for MVP but creates friction for multi-store buyers and makes re-engagement impossible (no email address tied to an account for marketing).
    59вҶ’
    60вҶ’3. **No notifications.** No email confirmations, no pickup reminders, no subscription renewal notices. Buyers and sellers have no communication channel through the platform. This will cause missed pickups and confusion.
    61вҶ’
    62вҶ’4. **No search or discovery.** The `/stores` page is a flat list. There is no location-based search, no filtering, no map. Buyers cannot find nearby farms. The primary acquisition channel (farmstand QR) sidesteps this, but it limits organic marketplace growth.
    63вҶ’
    64вҶ’5. **No seller analytics.** Sellers have a payout summary per window but no trends, subscriber growth, retention, or revenue over time. Farmers need to see their business growing to stay motivated.
    65вҶ’
    66вҶ’6. **One-time orders are incomplete.** The domain model supports one-time orders (pay-at-pickup), but there is no card checkout for one-time purchases. This leaves value on the table at pickup windows where non-subscribers want to buy.
    67вҶ’
    68вҶ’7. **No onboarding content or help.** No tooltips, no empty-state guidance beyond basic prompts. A first-time farmer will need hand-holding.
    69вҶ’
    70вҶ’---
    71вҶ’
    72вҶ’## 2. MVP Feature Prioritization (MoSCoW)
    73вҶ’
    74вҶ’The goal: validate product-market fit with 5-10 real farmers and their buyers within the first market season.
    75вҶ’
    76вҶ’### Must Have (ship before first real farmer)
    77вҶ’
    78вҶ’| Feature | Rationale |
    79вҶ’|---|---|
    80вҶ’| **Stripe Connect seller payouts** | Without this, the product does not function as a marketplace. Sellers must receive their money. Use Express onboarding for simplicity. |
    81вҶ’| **Email notifications (transactional)** | Subscription confirmation, pickup reminder (24h before), order ready notification. Minimum viable communication. Use a transactional provider (Resend, Postmark, or SES). |
    82вҶ’| **Buyer signup/login (lightweight)** | Even email-only magic link auth. Buyers need a single place to see all their subscriptions. Also enables re-engagement and reduces token-URL fragility. |
    83вҶ’
    84вҶ’### Should Have (ship within first 4-6 weeks of live usage)
    85вҶ’
    86вҶ’| Feature | Rationale |
    87вҶ’|---|---|
    88вҶ’| **Location-based store discovery** | Let buyers search by zip code or city. The lat/lng columns already exist on pickup_locations. Add a PostGIS radius query or simple bounding box filter. |
    89вҶ’| **One-time card checkout** | Enable walk-up or browse-only buyers to pay by card for a single order. Captures revenue from non-subscribers. |
    90вҶ’| **Seller analytics dashboard** | Subscriber count over time, revenue per cycle, retention rate. Keep it simple: 3-4 key metrics. Sellers need to see the value of the platform. |
    91вҶ’| **Pickup reminder push (SMS or email)** | Reduce no-show rate. A reminder the day before pickup is the single highest-leverage retention mechanism. |
    92вҶ’
    93вҶ’### Could Have (Phase 2, after initial PMF signal)
    94вҶ’
    95вҶ’| Feature | Rationale |
    96вҶ’|---|---|
    97вҶ’| **Box customization** | Let buyers choose/swap items within a box. 70% of CSA members prefer customizable boxes (industry data). Significantly increases retention. |
    98вҶ’| **Seller messaging / updates** | Let sellers post weekly notes ("This week's box includes..."). Builds trust and anticipation. |
    99вҶ’| **Referral program** | Give subscribers a referral link with a small credit. Word-of-mouth is the primary growth channel for local food. |
   100вҶ’| **Multi-location support** | Some farmers sell at multiple markets. Allow one plan to have multiple pickup locations. |
   101вҶ’
   102вҶ’### Won't Have (not now)
   103вҶ’
   104вҶ’| Feature | Rationale |
   105вҶ’|---|---|
   106вҶ’| **Delivery** | Local pickup only is the right constraint. Delivery adds massive ops complexity (routing, cold chain, driver management). |
   107вҶ’| **Wholesale / restaurant sales** | Different buyer persona, different pricing, different fulfillment. Stay focused on direct-to-consumer. |
   108вҶ’| **Mobile app** | The web app works on mobile already. A native app adds dev cost without proportional value at this stage. |
   109вҶ’| **AI-driven crop planning** | Interesting for scale, irrelevant for PMF validation. |
   110вҶ’
   111вҶ’---
   112вҶ’
   113вҶ’## 3. User Personas
   114вҶ’
   115вҶ’### Farmer Personas
   116вҶ’
   117вҶ’**Persona 1: Sarah -- The Saturday Market Farmer**
   118вҶ’- 32, runs a 5-acre organic vegetable operation outside Austin, TX
   119вҶ’- Sells at 2 farmers markets per week, grossing $1,200/week in peak season
   120вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   121вҶ’  - Convert one-time market customers into recurring revenue so she has predictable income
   122вҶ’  - Reduce waste by knowing how many boxes to pack before harvest
   123вҶ’  - Spend less time on logistics (payment collection, customer communication) and more time farming
   124вҶ’- Pain points: Chasing Venmo payments, no-shows who reserved produce, hand-written sign-up sheets that get lost
   125вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: The farmstand QR turns her busiest market day into a subscriber acquisition event. She prints a poster, and walk-up buyers become weekly subscribers with card on file.
   126вҶ’
   127вҶ’**Persona 2: Mike -- The Established CSA Operator**
   128вҶ’- 48, runs a 20-acre diversified farm in the Hudson Valley, NY
   129вҶ’- Has run a CSA for 8 years with 80 members. Uses spreadsheets and email chains.
   130вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   131вҶ’  - Modernize his CSA without losing the personal relationship with members
   132вҶ’  - Reduce administrative overhead (tracking payments, managing member communication)
   133вҶ’  - Grow beyond 80 members without proportionally increasing admin work
   134вҶ’- Pain points: Members who forget pickup days, manual payment tracking, no easy way for members to pause/resume
   135вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: Replaces his spreadsheets with a system that handles payments, sends reminders, and lets members self-manage their subscriptions.
   136вҶ’
   137вҶ’**Persona 3: Elena -- The Hobby-Scale Homesteader**
   138вҶ’- 28, grows specialty produce (microgreens, heirloom tomatoes) on 0.5 acres in a suburban backyard in Portland, OR
   139вҶ’- Sells informally via Instagram DMs and a neighborhood WhatsApp group
   140вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   141вҶ’  - Formalize her sales without the overhead of a "real" business platform
   142вҶ’  - Offer a simple subscription to her most loyal customers
   143вҶ’  - Look professional to attract new customers beyond her social circle
   144вҶ’- Pain points: Managing orders via DMs is chaotic, feels awkward asking friends to pay on time
   145вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: Dead-simple setup. She creates a box, shares the link on Instagram, and buyers subscribe with a card. No invoicing, no chasing payments.
   146вҶ’
   147вҶ’### Buyer Personas
   148вҶ’
   149вҶ’**Persona 1: Jess -- The Intentional Consumer**
   150вҶ’- 35, works remotely as a UX designer in Denver, CO
   151вҶ’- Shops at the farmers market most Saturdays, spends $40-60/week on produce
   152вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   153вҶ’  - Support local farmers in a meaningful, ongoing way (not just transactional)
   154вҶ’  - Get fresh, seasonal produce without the decision fatigue of shopping
   155вҶ’  - Have a reliable weekly rhythm -- know what she's getting, when, and where
   156вҶ’- Pain points: Forgets which farmers she liked, inconsistent availability at markets, guilt when she skips a week and "her farmer" doesn't get paid
   157вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: She scans a QR at the market, subscribes in 60 seconds, and gets a weekly box with pickup reminders. She can pause when she travels.
   158вҶ’
   159вҶ’**Persona 2: David -- The Busy Family Shopper**
   160вҶ’- 41, two kids, works as an accountant in suburban Charlotte, NC
   161вҶ’- Wants fresh produce but rarely makes it to the market
   162вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   163вҶ’  - Feed his family better without adding another errand to the weekend
   164вҶ’  - Know the cost upfront (no surprise grocery bills)
   165вҶ’  - Pickup on a schedule that fits his routine
   166вҶ’- Pain points: CSAs he's tried felt rigid -- all-or-nothing for a full season, paid upfront, couldn't pause
   167вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: No long-term commitment. Subscribe week by week. Pause or cancel anytime. Pickup location is on his commute.
   168вҶ’
   169вҶ’**Persona 3: Aisha -- The Neighborhood Connector**
   170вҶ’- 55, retired teacher, active in her community garden in Atlanta, GA
   171вҶ’- Buys from multiple local producers (eggs, honey, bread, vegetables)
   172вҶ’- Jobs to be done:
   173вҶ’  - Keep track of her various local food subscriptions in one place
   174вҶ’  - Share great farmers with friends and neighbors
   175вҶ’  - Feel connected to the people growing her food
   176вҶ’- Pain points: Juggles multiple Venmo subscriptions, spreadsheet tracking, different pickup days
   177вҶ’- Why Local-Roots: One platform for all her local subscriptions. Reviews and QR sharing let her evangelize to neighbors.
   178вҶ’
   179вҶ’---
   180вҶ’
   181вҶ’## 4. Key Differentiators
   182вҶ’
   183вҶ’### Local-Roots vs. Traditional CSAs
   184вҶ’
   185вҶ’| Dimension | Traditional CSA | Local-Roots |
   186вҶ’|---|---|---|
   187вҶ’| Commitment | Seasonal upfront payment ($400-800) | Subscribe per cycle, pause/cancel anytime |
   188вҶ’| Payment | Check, Venmo, cash at signup | Card on file, auto-authorized per pickup |
   189вҶ’| Flexibility | Fixed share, no customization | Flexible cadence (weekly/biweekly/monthly) |
   190вҶ’| Management | Spreadsheets, email chains | Self-service dashboard for sellers and buyers |
   191вҶ’| No-show handling | Lost produce, no recourse | Configurable no-show fee with auto-capture |
   192вҶ’| Acquisition | Word of mouth, website signup | Farmstand QR + digital discovery |
   193вҶ’
   194вҶ’### Local-Roots vs. Existing Platforms (Local Line, Farmigo, GrazeCert)
   195вҶ’
   196вҶ’| Dimension | Existing Platforms | Local-Roots |
   197вҶ’|---|---|---|
   198вҶ’| Pricing model | Monthly SaaS fee ($50-300/mo) | Free for sellers. 5% buyer service fee. |
   199вҶ’| Core focus | General farm e-commerce (one-time, wholesale, delivery, CSA) | Subscription-first, pickup-only. Opinionated and simple. |
   200вҶ’| Seller onboarding | Multi-step setup, feature-heavy dashboards | 3-step guided setup. Live in 5 minutes. |
   201вҶ’| Buyer acquisition | Seller drives all traffic to their store | Farmstand QR converts in-person impulse buyers + marketplace discovery |
   202вҶ’| Payment model | Seller pays for the platform | Buyer pays service fee. Sellers have zero cost to try. |
   203вҶ’| Fulfillment | Varies (delivery, shipping, pickup) | Pickup-only with code-verified handoff. No delivery ops. |
   204вҶ’
   205вҶ’### Core Differentiators (summary)
   206вҶ’
   207вҶ’1. **Zero seller cost.** Seller pays nothing. The buyer service fee funds the platform. This eliminates the #1 barrier to farmer adoption: "I can't afford another subscription tool."
   208вҶ’
   209вҶ’2. **Subscription-first, not subscription-added.** The entire UX is built around recurring boxes. It's not a general store with subscriptions bolted on. Every screen reinforces the recurring relationship.
   210вҶ’
   211вҶ’3. **Farmstand QR as acquisition channel.** This is unique. No competitor offers a printed QR that converts walk-up market customers into digital subscribers in 60 seconds. It bridges the physical-digital gap that plagues local food.
   212вҶ’
   213вҶ’4. **Pickup-only with code verification.** No delivery logistics. The 6-digit code + QR scan creates a verified handoff that protects both seller and buyer. Simple, trustworthy, low-cost.
   214вҶ’
   215вҶ’5. **Flexible commitment.** Unlike seasonal CSAs that demand hundreds upfront, Local-Roots lets buyers subscribe per-cycle with pause/cancel anytime. This lowers the bar for new subscribers and reduces the anxiety of commitment.
   216вҶ’
   217вҶ’---
   218вҶ’
   219вҶ’## 5. Product Roadmap
   220вҶ’
   221вҶ’### Phase 1: Validate (Weeks 1-8) -- "Can we get 5 farmers paid?"
   222вҶ’
   223вҶ’**Goal:** Get 5-10 real farmers live, each with 5+ paying subscribers, and successfully process end-to-end payments.
   224вҶ’
   225вҶ’**Must-ship features:**
   226вҶ’
   227вҶ’1. **Stripe Connect Express onboarding** (Week 1-2)
   228вҶ’   - Seller dashboard prompts Stripe Connect setup before going live
   229вҶ’   - Automatic payout after pickup confirmation (T+2 or weekly batch)
   230вҶ’   - Payout summary shows actual vs. estimated earnings
   231вҶ’
   232вҶ’2. **Transactional email notifications** (Week 2-3)
   233вҶ’   - Subscription confirmation email with link to manage
   234вҶ’   - Pickup reminder (24h before window opens)
   235вҶ’   - Order ready notification (when seller marks ready)
   236вҶ’   - Payment receipt after capture
   237вҶ’
   238вҶ’3. **Buyer authentication (magic link)** (Week 3-4)
   239вҶ’   - Email-based magic link login (no passwords)
   240вҶ’   - Buyer dashboard: list of active subscriptions, upcoming pickups, order history
   241вҶ’   - Migrate from token-only access to authenticated access (keep token as fallback)
   242вҶ’
   243вҶ’4. **Hardening and polish** (Week 4-6)
   244вҶ’   - Error handling audit across all flows
   245вҶ’   - Loading states, empty states, edge case messaging
   246вҶ’   - Mobile responsiveness audit (most buyers will use phones at the market)
   247вҶ’
   248вҶ’5. **Seed farmer onboarding** (Week 6-8)
   249вҶ’   - Recruit 5-10 farmers through direct outreach (farmers markets, local ag networks)
   250вҶ’   - White-glove onboarding: help them set up stores, print QR posters
   251вҶ’   - Collect qualitative feedback weekly
   252вҶ’
   253вҶ’**PMF signal:** At least 3 farmers renew usage after their first 4 pickup cycles. At least 50% of their subscribers complete 3+ pickups.
   254вҶ’
   255вҶ’### Phase 2: Grow (Months 3-6) -- "Can buyers find us without a QR code?"
   256вҶ’
   257вҶ’**Goal:** Organic buyer acquisition beyond farmstand QR. 50+ farmers, 500+ active subscribers.
   258вҶ’
   259вҶ’**Features:**
   260вҶ’
   261вҶ’1. **Location-based store discovery**
   262вҶ’   - Zip code / city search on the buyer-facing stores page
   263вҶ’   - Map view using lat/lng from pickup_locations
   264вҶ’   - Filter by cadence, price range
   265вҶ’
   266вҶ’2. **One-time card checkout**
   267вҶ’   - Buyers who aren't ready to subscribe can buy a single box with card payment
   268вҶ’   - Upsell to subscription after checkout ("Subscribe and save 10%")
   269вҶ’
   270вҶ’3. **Seller analytics dashboard**
   271вҶ’   - Subscriber count, churn rate, revenue per cycle (trailing 4 weeks)
   272вҶ’   - Top-performing boxes, no-show rate
   273вҶ’   - Simple charts, no enterprise complexity
   274вҶ’
   275вҶ’4. **Box preview / what's in the box**
   276вҶ’   - Sellers post a weekly note or photo of what's in the upcoming box
   277вҶ’   - Displayed on the buyer-facing box page and in the pickup reminder email
   278вҶ’   - Builds anticipation and reduces churn
   279вҶ’
   280вҶ’5. **Referral program (simple)**
   281вҶ’   - Subscribers get a shareable link
   282вҶ’   - New subscriber from referral gives both parties a small credit ($5)
   283вҶ’   - Track referral source for seller analytics
   284вҶ’
   285вҶ’6. **SEO and landing pages**
   286вҶ’   - `/farms/[city]` pages for local search traffic
   287вҶ’   - Store profile pages with reviews, photos, location
   288вҶ’   - Blog content: "Best farm boxes in [city]"
   289вҶ’
   290вҶ’### Phase 3: Scale (Months 6-12) -- "Can this be a real business?"
   291вҶ’
   292вҶ’**Goal:** 200+ farmers, 5,000+ active subscribers, path to $1M GMV run rate.
   293вҶ’
   294вҶ’**Features:**
   295вҶ’
   296вҶ’1. **Box customization**
   297вҶ’   - Buyers choose/swap items within a box (with seller-defined constraints)
   298вҶ’   - Increases retention by 25% based on industry data
   299вҶ’
   300вҶ’2. **Multi-location support**
   301вҶ’   - Farmers with multiple market locations can offer the same plan at different pickup points
   302вҶ’   - Buyers choose their preferred location
   303вҶ’
   304вҶ’3. **Seller CRM and communication**
   305вҶ’   - Seller can send messages to all subscribers (weekly update, seasonal note)
   306вҶ’   - Subscriber segments (new, loyal, at-risk)
   307вҶ’
   308вҶ’4. **Payment flexibility**
   309вҶ’   - Prepaid bundles (buy 4 pickups, get 1 free)
   310вҶ’   - Gift subscriptions
   311вҶ’   - EBT/SNAP acceptance (partnership-dependent, important for food access)
   312вҶ’
   313вҶ’5. **Food hub / multi-vendor boxes**
   314вҶ’   - Allow a food hub or market manager to create a multi-vendor box
   315вҶ’   - One box, multiple farms contributing items
   316вҶ’   - Hub takes a cut, each farmer gets paid their portion via Connect
   317вҶ’
   318вҶ’6. **API for integrations**
   319вҶ’   - Public API for farmers to integrate with POS systems, accounting tools
   320вҶ’   - Webhook notifications for third-party integrations
   321вҶ’
   322вҶ’---
   323вҶ’
   324вҶ’## 6. Success Metrics
   325вҶ’
   326вҶ’### Phase 1 KPIs (Validate)
   327вҶ’
   328вҶ’| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
   329вҶ’|---|---|---|
   330вҶ’| Live farmers | 5-10 | Enough to test diverse use cases (scale, crop type, market type) |
   331вҶ’| Active subscribers per farmer | 5+ | Minimum viable subscriber base to test fulfillment flow |
   332вҶ’| Subscriber pickup rate | >80% | Subscribers actually show up. Below 80% signals a product or reminder problem. |
   333вҶ’| Seller weekly active rate | >80% | Sellers are using the dashboard and fulfilling orders, not abandoning the tool. |
   334вҶ’| Time to first live box | <30 min | Onboarding friction. If it takes longer, the setup flow needs simplification. |
   335вҶ’| NPS (seller) | >40 | Sellers recommend the platform to other farmers. |
   336вҶ’| Gross payment volume | Track only | Too early for a target, but establishes baseline. |
   337вҶ’
   338вҶ’### Phase 2 KPIs (Grow)
   339вҶ’
   340вҶ’| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
   341вҶ’|---|---|---|
   342вҶ’| Monthly farmer signups | 10-15 | Organic inbound without paid acquisition |
   343вҶ’| Subscriber growth rate | 15-20% MoM | Compound growth from QR, referral, and discovery |
   344вҶ’| Subscriber retention (4-week) | >65% | Subscribers who stay past 4 cycles are high-LTV |
   345вҶ’| One-time to subscription conversion | >15% | One-time buyers convert to subscribers (validates upsell) |
   346вҶ’| Referral rate | >10% | Subscribers who refer at least one new subscriber |
   347вҶ’| GMV per farmer per month | $500+ | Revenue threshold where the platform is meaningful to the farmer |
   348вҶ’| Platform take rate | ~5% | Stable buyer fee with no seller churn due to pricing |
   349вҶ’
   350вҶ’### Phase 3 KPIs (Scale)
   351вҶ’
   352вҶ’| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
   353вҶ’|---|---|---|
   354вҶ’| Total GMV (monthly) | $80K+ | Path to $1M annual GMV |
   355вҶ’| Active farmers | 200+ | Geographic density matters for marketplace effects |
   356вҶ’| Active subscribers | 5,000+ | Critical mass for word-of-mouth growth |
   357вҶ’| Subscriber LTV | >$200 | Average revenue per subscriber over their lifetime |
   358вҶ’| CAC (blended) | <$15 | Cost to acquire a subscriber (should be very low given QR + referral) |
   359вҶ’| LTV:CAC ratio | >10:1 | Healthy unit economics for a marketplace |
   360вҶ’| Seller churn (monthly) | <5% | Farmers stay because the platform makes them money |
   361вҶ’| No-show rate | <10% | Reminders and payment commitment reduce no-shows |
   362вҶ’
   363вҶ’### North Star Metric
   364вҶ’
   365вҶ’**Completed pickups per week.** This single metric captures the health of both sides of the marketplace. It requires sellers to be live, subscribers to be active, and fulfillment to work. If completed pickups per week is growing, the product is working.
   366вҶ’
   367вҶ’---
   368вҶ’
   369вҶ’## Appendix: Competitive Landscape Reference
   370вҶ’
   371вҶ’- **Local Line**: Leading farm e-commerce platform. SaaS model ($50-300/mo). General-purpose (CSA, one-time, wholesale, delivery). Strong product but expensive for small farmers. Farms using Local Line grew sales 33% YoY in 2025.
   372вҶ’- **Farmigo**: Farm-to-consumer marketplace with CSA support. Community-focused. Less actively developed.
   373вҶ’- **GrazeCert / GrazeCart**: E-commerce for farms, focused on meat and specialty producers. Delivery-oriented.
   374вҶ’- **Harvie**: CSA management platform. Closed in 2025. Farms migrating to Local Line.
   375вҶ’- **Local Food Marketplace**: Comprehensive platform for farms, food hubs, CSAs. Multi-channel (DTC + wholesale).
   376вҶ’- **Generic e-commerce (Shopify, Square)**: Farms use these but lack subscription management, pickup workflow, and farmstand QR. Not purpose-built.
   377вҶ’
   378вҶ’The key gap Local-Roots fills: **a zero-cost, subscription-first platform for farmers who sell at pickup (markets, farmstands) and want to convert walk-up buyers into recurring subscribers.** No competitor targets this exact wedge with this pricing model.
   379вҶ’

file.content:
# Local-Roots Product Strategy

## 1. Current Product Audit

### What Is Built

Local-Roots is a local pickup marketplace with a functional end-to-end flow for recurring farm box subscriptions.

**Seller side (strong):**
- Registration and authentication (JWT-based)
- Store creation (one store per seller)
- Pickup location setup with Google Places autocomplete and timezone handling
- Subscription plan creation: title, cadence (weekly/biweekly/monthly), price, subscriber cap, first pickup date
- Cycle generation: creates pickup windows, offerings, and orders for active subscribers automatically
- Order fulfillment dashboard: mark ready, confirm pickup with 6-digit code (manual entry or QR scan), no-show handling with configurable fee
- Farmstand QR code generation and printable poster for in-person marketing
- Pause/resume box plans
- Payout summary per pickup window (estimated seller payout, platform fee, no-show fees)
- Store settings page

**Buyer side (functional):**
- Browse stores, view boxes, subscribe via Stripe PaymentElement
- Subscription management: pause, resume, cancel, update payment card
- Order page with pickup code display and QR code for seller scan
- Post-pickup reviews (1-5 rating + comment, unlocked after pickup confirmation)
- Token-based access (no buyer accounts -- frictionless but limited)

**Payments (partially complete):**
- Stripe SetupIntent for future pickups (>7 days out), PaymentIntent with manual capture for near-term pickups
- Webhook-driven payment status synchronization
- Capture on pickup confirmation, partial capture for no-show fees
- 5% buyer service fee
- One-time orders exist at pay-at-pickup only (no card checkout for one-time)

**Infrastructure:**
- Next.js (App Router) frontend on Vercel
- Go API backend on Railway with PostgreSQL
- Well-structured schema: 12 migrations, proper constraints, composite foreign keys, concurrency-safe inventory reservation

### Strengths

1. **Subscription-first architecture.** The entire data model -- subscription plans, cycles, recurring orders -- is purpose-built for the recurring box use case. This is the right wedge; most competitors bolt subscriptions onto generic e-commerce.

2. **Low seller setup friction.** The guided setup flow (location -> box -> review -> go live) gets a farmer from registration to accepting subscribers in minutes. Google Places autocomplete eliminates manual address entry.

3. **Farmstand QR bridge.** This is a genuinely clever acquisition channel. A farmer prints a QR poster, tapes it to their booth, and walk-up market customers become recurring subscribers. This converts a one-time impulse buy into a subscription relationship -- the exact behavior that makes the unit economics work.

4. **Fulfillment completeness.** The ready -> pickup code -> confirm flow with QR scanning is operationally sound. No-show handling with fee/waive options shows mature thinking about edge cases.

5. **Payment architecture.** The SetupIntent / PaymentIntent split with manual capture is correct for this domain. Authorize early, capture on fulfillment. This protects both sides.

6. **Schema quality.** Composite foreign keys, concurrency guards on inventory, proper constraint checks -- this will scale without data integrity issues.

### Weaknesses

1. **No seller payouts.** Stripe Connect is not integrated. Sellers cannot actually receive money. This is the single biggest blocker to real-world usage. A farmer will not use the platform if money stays in the platform's Stripe account indefinitely.

2. **No buyer accounts.** Buyers access subscriptions and orders via token-based URLs. There is no login, no dashboard, no way to see all subscriptions in one place. This works for MVP but creates friction for multi-store buyers and makes re-engagement impossible (no email address tied to an account for marketing).

3. **No notifications.** No email confirmations, no pickup reminders, no subscription renewal notices. Buyers and sellers have no communication channel through the platform. This will cause missed pickups and confusion.

4. **No search or discovery.** The `/stores` page is a flat list. There is no location-based search, no filtering, no map. Buyers cannot find nearby farms. The primary acquisition channel (farmstand QR) sidesteps this, but it limits organic marketplace growth.

5. **No seller analytics.** Sellers have a payout summary per window but no trends, subscriber growth, retention, or revenue over time. Farmers need to see their business growing to stay motivated.

6. **One-time orders are incomplete.** The domain model supports one-time orders (pay-at-pickup), but there is no card checkout for one-time purchases. This leaves value on the table at pickup windows where non-subscribers want to buy.

7. **No onboarding content or help.** No tooltips, no empty-state guidance beyond basic prompts. A first-time farmer will need hand-holding.

---

## 2. MVP Feature Prioritization (MoSCoW)

The goal: validate product-market fit with 5-10 real farmers and their buyers within the first market season.

### Must Have (ship before first real farmer)

| Feature | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Stripe Connect seller payouts** | Without this, the product does not function as a marketplace. Sellers must receive their money. Use Express onboarding for simplicity. |
| **Email notifications (transactional)** | Subscription confirmation, pickup reminder (24h before), order ready notification. Minimum viable communication. Use a transactional provider (Resend, Postmark, or SES). |
| **Buyer signup/login (lightweight)** | Even email-only magic link auth. Buyers need a single place to see all their subscriptions. Also enables re-engagement and reduces token-URL fragility. |

### Should Have (ship within first 4-6 weeks of live usage)

| Feature | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Location-based store discovery** | Let buyers search by zip code or city. The lat/lng columns already exist on pickup_locations. Add a PostGIS radius query or simple bounding box filter. |
| **One-time card checkout** | Enable walk-up or browse-only buyers to pay by card for a single order. Captures revenue from non-subscribers. |
| **Seller analytics dashboard** | Subscriber count over time, revenue per cycle, retention rate. Keep it simple: 3-4 key metrics. Sellers need to see the value of the platform. |
| **Pickup reminder push (SMS or email)** | Reduce no-show rate. A reminder the day before pickup is the single highest-leverage retention mechanism. |

### Could Have (Phase 2, after initial PMF signal)

| Feature | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Box customization** | Let buyers choose/swap items within a box. 70% of CSA members prefer customizable boxes (industry data). Significantly increases retention. |
| **Seller messaging / updates** | Let sellers post weekly notes ("This week's box includes..."). Builds trust and anticipation. |
| **Referral program** | Give subscribers a referral link with a small credit. Word-of-mouth is the primary growth channel for local food. |
| **Multi-location support** | Some farmers sell at multiple markets. Allow one plan to have multiple pickup locations. |

### Won't Have (not now)

| Feature | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Delivery** | Local pickup only is the right constraint. Delivery adds massive ops complexity (routing, cold chain, driver management). |
| **Wholesale / restaurant sales** | Different buyer persona, different pricing, different fulfillment. Stay focused on direct-to-consumer. |
| **Mobile app** | The web app works on mobile already. A native app adds dev cost without proportional value at this stage. |
| **AI-driven crop planning** | Interesting for scale, irrelevant for PMF validation. |

---

## 3. User Personas

### Farmer Personas

**Persona 1: Sarah -- The Saturday Market Farmer**
- 32, runs a 5-acre organic vegetable operation outside Austin, TX
- Sells at 2 farmers markets per week, grossing $1,200/week in peak season
- Jobs to be done:
  - Convert one-time market customers into recurring revenue so she has predictable income
  - Reduce waste by knowing how many boxes to pack before harvest
  - Spend less time on logistics (payment collection, customer communication) and more time farming
- Pain points: Chasing Venmo payments, no-shows who reserved produce, hand-written sign-up sheets that get lost
- Why Local-Roots: The farmstand QR turns her busiest market day into a subscriber acquisition event. She prints a poster, and walk-up buyers become weekly subscribers with card on file.

**Persona 2: Mike -- The Established CSA Operator**
- 48, runs a 20-acre diversified farm in the Hudson Valley, NY
- Has run a CSA for 8 years with 80 members. Uses spreadsheets and email chains.
- Jobs to be done:
  - Modernize his CSA without losing the personal relationship with members
  - Reduce administrative overhead (tracking payments, managing member communication)
  - Grow beyond 80 members without proportionally increasing admin work
- Pain points: Members who forget pickup days, manual payment tracking, no easy way for members to pause/resume
- Why Local-Roots: Replaces his spreadsheets with a system that handles payments, sends reminders, and lets members self-manage their subscriptions.

**Persona 3: Elena -- The Hobby-Scale Homesteader**
- 28, grows specialty produce (microgreens, heirloom tomatoes) on 0.5 acres in a suburban backyard in Portland, OR
- Sells informally via Instagram DMs and a neighborhood WhatsApp group
- Jobs to be done:
  - Formalize her sales without the overhead of a "real" business platform
  - Offer a simple subscription to her most loyal customers
  - Look professional to attract new customers beyond her social circle
- Pain points: Managing orders via DMs is chaotic, feels awkward asking friends to pay on time
- Why Local-Roots: Dead-simple setup. She creates a box, shares the link on Instagram, and buyers subscribe with a card. No invoicing, no chasing payments.

### Buyer Personas

**Persona 1: Jess -- The Intentional Consumer**
- 35, works remotely as a UX designer in Denver, CO
- Shops at the farmers market most Saturdays, spends $40-60/week on produce
- Jobs to be done:
  - Support local farmers in a meaningful, ongoing way (not just transactional)
  - Get fresh, seasonal produce without the decision fatigue of shopping
  - Have a reliable weekly rhythm -- know what she's getting, when, and where
- Pain points: Forgets which farmers she liked, inconsistent availability at markets, guilt when she skips a week and "her farmer" doesn't get paid
- Why Local-Roots: She scans a QR at the market, subscribes in 60 seconds, and gets a weekly box with pickup reminders. She can pause when she travels.

**Persona 2: David -- The Busy Family Shopper**
- 41, two kids, works as an accountant in suburban Charlotte, NC
- Wants fresh produce but rarely makes it to the market
- Jobs to be done:
  - Feed his family better without adding another errand to the weekend
  - Know the cost upfront (no surprise grocery bills)
  - Pickup on a schedule that fits his routine
- Pain points: CSAs he's tried felt rigid -- all-or-nothing for a full season, paid upfront, couldn't pause
- Why Local-Roots: No long-term commitment. Subscribe week by week. Pause or cancel anytime. Pickup location is on his commute.

**Persona 3: Aisha -- The Neighborhood Connector**
- 55, retired teacher, active in her community garden in Atlanta, GA
- Buys from multiple local producers (eggs, honey, bread, vegetables)
- Jobs to be done:
  - Keep track of her various local food subscriptions in one place
  - Share great farmers with friends and neighbors
  - Feel connected to the people growing her food
- Pain points: Juggles multiple Venmo subscriptions, spreadsheet tracking, different pickup days
- Why Local-Roots: One platform for all her local subscriptions. Reviews and QR sharing let her evangelize to neighbors.

---

## 4. Key Differentiators

### Local-Roots vs. Traditional CSAs

| Dimension | Traditional CSA | Local-Roots |
|---|---|---|
| Commitment | Seasonal upfront payment ($400-800) | Subscribe per cycle, pause/cancel anytime |
| Payment | Check, Venmo, cash at signup | Card on file, auto-authorized per pickup |
| Flexibility | Fixed share, no customization | Flexible cadence (weekly/biweekly/monthly) |
| Management | Spreadsheets, email chains | Self-service dashboard for sellers and buyers |
| No-show handling | Lost produce, no recourse | Configurable no-show fee with auto-capture |
| Acquisition | Word of mouth, website signup | Farmstand QR + digital discovery |

### Local-Roots vs. Existing Platforms (Local Line, Farmigo, GrazeCert)

| Dimension | Existing Platforms | Local-Roots |
|---|---|---|
| Pricing model | Monthly SaaS fee ($50-300/mo) | Free for sellers. 5% buyer service fee. |
| Core focus | General farm e-commerce (one-time, wholesale, delivery, CSA) | Subscription-first, pickup-only. Opinionated and simple. |
| Seller onboarding | Multi-step setup, feature-heavy dashboards | 3-step guided setup. Live in 5 minutes. |
| Buyer acquisition | Seller drives all traffic to their store | Farmstand QR converts in-person impulse buyers + marketplace discovery |
| Payment model | Seller pays for the platform | Buyer pays service fee. Sellers have zero cost to try. |
| Fulfillment | Varies (delivery, shipping, pickup) | Pickup-only with code-verified handoff. No delivery ops. |

### Core Differentiators (summary)

1. **Zero seller cost.** Seller pays nothing. The buyer service fee funds the platform. This eliminates the #1 barrier to farmer adoption: "I can't afford another subscription tool."

2. **Subscription-first, not subscription-added.** The entire UX is built around recurring boxes. It's not a general store with subscriptions bolted on. Every screen reinforces the recurring relationship.

3. **Farmstand QR as acquisition channel.** This is unique. No competitor offers a printed QR that converts walk-up market customers into digital subscribers in 60 seconds. It bridges the physical-digital gap that plagues local food.

4. **Pickup-only with code verification.** No delivery logistics. The 6-digit code + QR scan creates a verified handoff that protects both seller and buyer. Simple, trustworthy, low-cost.

5. **Flexible commitment.** Unlike seasonal CSAs that demand hundreds upfront, Local-Roots lets buyers subscribe per-cycle with pause/cancel anytime. This lowers the bar for new subscribers and reduces the anxiety of commitment.

---

## 5. Product Roadmap

### Phase 1: Validate (Weeks 1-8) -- "Can we get 5 farmers paid?"

**Goal:** Get 5-10 real farmers live, each with 5+ paying subscribers, and successfully process end-to-end payments.

**Must-ship features:**

1. **Stripe Connect Express onboarding** (Week 1-2)
   - Seller dashboard prompts Stripe Connect setup before going live
   - Automatic payout after pickup confirmation (T+2 or weekly batch)
   - Payout summary shows actual vs. estimated earnings

2. **Transactional email notifications** (Week 2-3)
   - Subscription confirmation email with link to manage
   - Pickup reminder (24h before window opens)
   - Order ready notification (when seller marks ready)
   - Payment receipt after capture

3. **Buyer authentication (magic link)** (Week 3-4)
   - Email-based magic link login (no passwords)
   - Buyer dashboard: list of active subscriptions, upcoming pickups, order history
   - Migrate from token-only access to authenticated access (keep token as fallback)

4. **Hardening and polish** (Week 4-6)
   - Error handling audit across all flows
   - Loading states, empty states, edge case messaging
   - Mobile responsiveness audit (most buyers will use phones at the market)

5. **Seed farmer onboarding** (Week 6-8)
   - Recruit 5-10 farmers through direct outreach (farmers markets, local ag networks)
   - White-glove onboarding: help them set up stores, print QR posters
   - Collect qualitative feedback weekly

**PMF signal:** At least 3 farmers renew usage after their first 4 pickup cycles. At least 50% of their subscribers complete 3+ pickups.

### Phase 2: Grow (Months 3-6) -- "Can buyers find us without a QR code?"

**Goal:** Organic buyer acquisition beyond farmstand QR. 50+ farmers, 500+ active subscribers.

**Features:**

1. **Location-based store discovery**
   - Zip code / city search on the buyer-facing stores page
   - Map view using lat/lng from pickup_locations
   - Filter by cadence, price range

2. **One-time card checkout**
   - Buyers who aren't ready to subscribe can buy a single box with card payment
   - Upsell to subscription after checkout ("Subscribe and save 10%")

3. **Seller analytics dashboard**
   - Subscriber count, churn rate, revenue per cycle (trailing 4 weeks)
   - Top-performing boxes, no-show rate
   - Simple charts, no enterprise complexity

4. **Box preview / what's in the box**
   - Sellers post a weekly note or photo of what's in the upcoming box
   - Displayed on the buyer-facing box page and in the pickup reminder email
   - Builds anticipation and reduces churn

5. **Referral program (simple)**
   - Subscribers get a shareable link
   - New subscriber from referral gives both parties a small credit ($5)
   - Track referral source for seller analytics

6. **SEO and landing pages**
   - `/farms/[city]` pages for local search traffic
   - Store profile pages with reviews, photos, location
   - Blog content: "Best farm boxes in [city]"

### Phase 3: Scale (Months 6-12) -- "Can this be a real business?"

**Goal:** 200+ farmers, 5,000+ active subscribers, path to $1M GMV run rate.

**Features:**

1. **Box customization**
   - Buyers choose/swap items within a box (with seller-defined constraints)
   - Increases retention by 25% based on industry data

2. **Multi-location support**
   - Farmers with multiple market locations can offer the same plan at different pickup points
   - Buyers choose their preferred location

3. **Seller CRM and communication**
   - Seller can send messages to all subscribers (weekly update, seasonal note)
   - Subscriber segments (new, loyal, at-risk)

4. **Payment flexibility**
   - Prepaid bundles (buy 4 pickups, get 1 free)
   - Gift subscriptions
   - EBT/SNAP acceptance (partnership-dependent, important for food access)

5. **Food hub / multi-vendor boxes**
   - Allow a food hub or market manager to create a multi-vendor box
   - One box, multiple farms contributing items
   - Hub takes a cut, each farmer gets paid their portion via Connect

6. **API for integrations**
   - Public API for farmers to integrate with POS systems, accounting tools
   - Webhook notifications for third-party integrations

---

## 6. Success Metrics

### Phase 1 KPIs (Validate)

| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Live farmers | 5-10 | Enough to test diverse use cases (scale, crop type, market type) |
| Active subscribers per farmer | 5+ | Minimum viable subscriber base to test fulfillment flow |
| Subscriber pickup rate | >80% | Subscribers actually show up. Below 80% signals a product or reminder problem. |
| Seller weekly active rate | >80% | Sellers are using the dashboard and fulfilling orders, not abandoning the tool. |
| Time to first live box | <30 min | Onboarding friction. If it takes longer, the setup flow needs simplification. |
| NPS (seller) | >40 | Sellers recommend the platform to other farmers. |
| Gross payment volume | Track only | Too early for a target, but establishes baseline. |

### Phase 2 KPIs (Grow)

| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Monthly farmer signups | 10-15 | Organic inbound without paid acquisition |
| Subscriber growth rate | 15-20% MoM | Compound growth from QR, referral, and discovery |
| Subscriber retention (4-week) | >65% | Subscribers who stay past 4 cycles are high-LTV |
| One-time to subscription conversion | >15% | One-time buyers convert to subscribers (validates upsell) |
| Referral rate | >10% | Subscribers who refer at least one new subscriber |
| GMV per farmer per month | $500+ | Revenue threshold where the platform is meaningful to the farmer |
| Platform take rate | ~5% | Stable buyer fee with no seller churn due to pricing |

### Phase 3 KPIs (Scale)

| Metric | Target | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Total GMV (monthly) | $80K+ | Path to $1M annual GMV |
| Active farmers | 200+ | Geographic density matters for marketplace effects |
| Active subscribers | 5,000+ | Critical mass for word-of-mouth growth |
| Subscriber LTV | >$200 | Average revenue per subscriber over their lifetime |
| CAC (blended) | <$15 | Cost to acquire a subscriber (should be very low given QR + referral) |
| LTV:CAC ratio | >10:1 | Healthy unit economics for a marketplace |
| Seller churn (monthly) | <5% | Farmers stay because the platform makes them money |
| No-show rate | <10% | Reminders and payment commitment reduce no-shows |

### North Star Metric

**Completed pickups per week.** This single metric captures the health of both sides of the marketplace. It requires sellers to be live, subscribers to be active, and fulfillment to work. If completed pickups per week is growing, the product is working.

---

## Appendix: Competitive Landscape Reference

- **Local Line**: Leading farm e-commerce platform. SaaS model ($50-300/mo). General-purpose (CSA, one-time, wholesale, delivery). Strong product but expensive for small farmers. Farms using Local Line grew sales 33% YoY in 2025.
- **Farmigo**: Farm-to-consumer marketplace with CSA support. Community-focused. Less actively developed.
- **GrazeCert / GrazeCart**: E-commerce for farms, focused on meat and specialty producers. Delivery-oriented.
- **Harvie**: CSA management platform. Closed in 2025. Farms migrating to Local Line.
- **Local Food Marketplace**: Comprehensive platform for farms, food hubs, CSAs. Multi-channel (DTC + wholesale).
- **Generic e-commerce (Shopify, Square)**: Farms use these but lack subscription management, pickup workflow, and farmstand QR. Not purpose-built.

The key gap Local-Roots fills: **a zero-cost, subscription-first platform for farmers who sell at pickup (markets, farmstands) and want to convert walk-up buyers into recurring subscribers.** No competitor targets this exact wedge with this pricing model.

file.content:
# Domain Model (MVP)

## Wedge

- Produce/food only
- Local pickup only
- Seller chooses pickup date/time/location + order cutoff
- One order is limited to one seller and one pickup window

## Core entities

- User
- Store (seller storefront)
- PickupLocation (address + timezone)
- PickupWindow (start/end + cutoff + location)
- Product (canonical item)
- Offering (product inventory scoped to a pickup window)
- Order + OrderItem (snapshots of offerings at time of purchase)
- Payment + Refund (future; Stripe references)
- Review (1 per order, only after fulfillment)

## Order state machine

Current implementation (Go backend + Postgres) uses:

- `placed` -> `ready` -> `picked_up`
- `placed` -> `canceled`
- `ready` -> `no_show`

Notes:

- `payment_method` is `card` (all orders require card payment).
- `payment_status` tracks payment lifecycle (`unpaid`/`authorized`/`paid`/`refunded`), driven by Stripe.

Rules:

- Order items cannot change after pickup is finalized (`picked_up`).
- Reviews only allowed for completed orders (`picked_up`).
- Offering quantity must not oversell under concurrency.

1вҶ’# CEO Strategy Memo: Local-Roots Path to Scale
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’**Date:** February 2026
     4вҶ’**Author:** CEO / Chief Strategist
     5вҶ’**Status:** Strategic Planning Document
     6вҶ’
     7вҶ’---
     8вҶ’
     9вҶ’## 1. Vision & Mission
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’### Mission
    12вҶ’Empower every local farmer to build a thriving direct-to-consumer subscription business with zero technical overhead.
    13вҶ’
    14вҶ’### Vision
    15вҶ’Local-Roots becomes the operating system for local food commerce -- the platform where every farmer in North America manages recurring revenue, and every family discovers trusted, local food within a 30-minute drive.
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’### The 10-Year Picture
    18вҶ’At scale, Local-Roots is a vertical commerce platform that owns the relationship layer between local food producers and consumers. We are to local food what Shopify is to online retail: the trusted infrastructure that makes selling simple and buying delightful. The difference is that our commerce is inherently local, recurring, and trust-driven -- which means we can build density-based network effects that Shopify cannot.
    19вҶ’
    20вҶ’**Tagline candidate:** "Your farmer, on repeat."
    21вҶ’
    22вҶ’---
    23вҶ’
    24вҶ’## 2. Business Model Canvas (Lean Canvas)
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’| Element | Description |
    27вҶ’|---|---|
    28вҶ’| **Problem** | Farmers waste 15-20 hours/week on manual subscription management, payment collection, and no-show logistics. Buyers want local food but find CSAs rigid and farmers markets unreliable. |
    29вҶ’| **Customer Segments** | (1) Small-to-mid-size farms doing $50K-$500K in direct sales. (2) Health-conscious urban/suburban families willing to pay premium for local, fresh food. |
    30вҶ’| **Unique Value Proposition** | Farmers get Shopify-simple subscription management with built-in demand. Buyers get one-tap access to local farm subscriptions with guaranteed pickup slots. |
    31вҶ’| **Solution** | Subscription box builder, Stripe-powered recurring billing, pickup window management, QR code fulfillment verification, buyer discovery marketplace. |
    32вҶ’| **Channels** | (1) Farmer onboarding via farmers market field sales, ag extension partnerships, word-of-mouth. (2) Buyer acquisition via local SEO, farmer cross-promotion, community partnerships. |
    33вҶ’| **Revenue Streams** | 7% + $0.35 buyer service fee on every transaction. Future: seller SaaS tier ($29-99/mo for advanced features), promoted listings, data/insights products. |
    34вҶ’| **Cost Structure** | Engineering (60%), farmer success/onboarding (20%), hosting and payment processing (10%), marketing (10%). |
    35вҶ’| **Key Metrics** | GMV, active subscriptions, farmer retention, buyer retention, subscription renewal rate, pickup completion rate. |
    36вҶ’| **Unfair Advantage** | Density-based supply lock-in: once a farmer's subscribers are on the platform, switching costs are high. Local trust graph is defensible and compounds. |
    37вҶ’
    38вҶ’---
    39вҶ’
    40вҶ’## 3. Unit Economics
    41вҶ’
    42вҶ’### Per-Transaction Economics
    43вҶ’
    44вҶ’> **Note:** All revenue figures below are **net of Stripe processing fees**, reflecting true platform contribution. Previous versions showed gross fees, which overstated unit economics.
    45вҶ’
    46вҶ’| Metric | Value | Notes |
    47вҶ’|---|---|---|
    48вҶ’| Average subscription box price | $45 | Based on CSA market data |
    49вҶ’| Buyer service fee (7% + $0.35) | $3.50 | Gross platform revenue per transaction |
    50вҶ’| Total charged to buyer | $48.50 | Subtotal + service fee |
    51вҶ’| Stripe processing (~2.9% + $0.30) | -$1.71 | Absorbed by platform out of service fee |
    52вҶ’| **Net platform revenue per transaction** | **$1.79** | Service fee minus Stripe |
    53вҶ’| Platform COGS per transaction | ~$0.15 | Hosting, support, infrastructure |
    54вҶ’| **Net contribution per transaction** | **$1.64** | Revenue after all variable costs |
    55вҶ’
    56вҶ’### Per-Farmer Economics
    57вҶ’
    58вҶ’| Metric | Value | Notes |
    59вҶ’|---|---|---|
    60вҶ’| Average subscribers per farmer | 40 | At steady state |
    61вҶ’| Weighted avg order frequency | 3.1x/month | 60% weekly, 25% biweekly, 15% monthly |
    62вҶ’| Monthly GMV per farmer | $5,580 | 40 subs Г— 3.1 orders Г— $45 |
    63вҶ’| Monthly net contribution per farmer | $213 | 40 subs Г— $5.33 blended contribution |
    64вҶ’| Annual net contribution per farmer | $2,558 | |
    65вҶ’| Farmer acquisition cost (CAC) | $300-500 | Field sales + onboarding support |
    66вҶ’| **Farmer LTV (3-yr, 80% retention)** | **$5,247** | Discounted at 10% annual rate, net of Stripe |
    67вҶ’| **LTV:CAC ratio** | **10.5-17.5x** | Based on net contribution (after Stripe fees) |
    68вҶ’
    69вҶ’### Per-Buyer Economics
    70вҶ’
    71вҶ’| Metric | Value | Notes |
    72вҶ’|---|---|---|
    73вҶ’| Monthly spend per active subscriber | $140 | 3.1 orders Г— $45 |
    74вҶ’| Monthly gross service fee per subscriber | $10.85 | 3.1 orders Г— $3.50 |
    75вҶ’| Monthly net contribution per subscriber | $5.33 | After Stripe fees and infrastructure |
    76вҶ’| Annual net contribution per subscriber | $63.96 | |
    77вҶ’| Buyer CAC | $8-15 | Organic/referral-heavy model |
    78вҶ’| **Subscriber LTV (12.5 mo avg lifetime, 8% churn)** | **$60** | Discounted at 10% annual rate |
    79вҶ’| **LTV:CAC ratio** | **4.0-7.5x** | Strong for marketplace; based on net contribution |
    80вҶ’
    81вҶ’### Break-Even Analysis
    82вҶ’
    83вҶ’> **Note:** "Monthly Net Contribution" is revenue after Stripe processing fees and infrastructure costs. Previous versions showed gross fees, which overstated unit economics.
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’| Scale | Farmers | Subscribers | Monthly GMV | Monthly Net Contribution | Monthly Costs | Status |
    86вҶ’|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
    87вҶ’| Pre-seed (now) | 20 | 200 | $28K | $1.1K | $15K | -$13.9K/mo |
    88вҶ’| Seed milestone | 200 | 4,000 | $558K | $21.3K | $40K | -$18.7K/mo |
    89вҶ’| Series A target | 1,000 | 25,000 | $3.5M | $133K | $80K | +$53K/mo |
    90вҶ’| Break-even (scale team) | 610 | 24,400 | $3.4M | $130K | $130K | ~$0/mo |
    91вҶ’| Scale (Year 4) | 10,000 | 300,000 | $41.9M | $1.6M | $400K | +$1.2M/mo |
    92вҶ’
    93вҶ’**Key insight:** At 7% + $0.35 take rate, net of Stripe fees, break-even at scale-team costs ($130K/mo) requires ~610 farmers with ~24,400 active subscribers -- a 74% reduction from the prior 5% model. Adding a seller SaaS tier ($49/mo average) at 30% attach rate accelerates break-even to ~400 farmers.
    94вҶ’
    95вҶ’### Revenue Model Evolution
    96вҶ’
    97вҶ’| Phase | Revenue Source | Contribution |
    98вҶ’|---|---|---|
    99вҶ’| Phase 1 (Now) | 7% + $0.35 buyer service fee | 100% |
   100вҶ’| Phase 2 (Year 2) | + Seller SaaS tier ($29-99/mo) | 60% / 40% |
   101вҶ’| Phase 3 (Year 3) | + Promoted listings, analytics | 45% / 35% / 20% |
   102вҶ’| Phase 4 (Year 4+) | + Financial services, insurance, logistics | Diversified |
   103вҶ’
   104вҶ’---
   105вҶ’
   106вҶ’## 4. Moat & Defensibility
   107вҶ’
   108вҶ’### 4.1 Local Density Network Effects
   109вҶ’
   110вҶ’Unlike global marketplaces, Local-Roots benefits from **density-based network effects** that are hard to replicate:
   111вҶ’
   112вҶ’- Each new farmer in a metro area makes the platform more valuable to every buyer in that area (more choice, more pickup windows, more variety).
   113вҶ’- Each new buyer makes the platform more valuable to every farmer (more demand, faster subscriber cap fill).
   114вҶ’- **Critical mass per market is low** (~15-20 farmers per metro) but once achieved, the marketplace becomes self-reinforcing.
   115вҶ’
   116вҶ’This is the "Uber playbook" applied to food: win city by city, and once you own local density, competitors face a cold-start problem in every market you dominate.
   117вҶ’
   118вҶ’### 4.2 Subscription Lock-In (Supply Side)
   119вҶ’
   120вҶ’Farmers using Local-Roots to manage recurring subscriptions face significant switching costs:
   121вҶ’
   122вҶ’- **Subscriber relationship migration:** Moving 40+ active subscribers to a new platform requires re-signup, re-entry of payment info, and risks subscriber churn.
   123вҶ’- **Operational workflow integration:** Pickup schedules, inventory planning, and fulfillment flows become embedded in daily operations.
   124вҶ’- **Historical data dependency:** Subscription analytics, seasonal demand patterns, and buyer preferences accumulate over time.
   125вҶ’
   126вҶ’This mirrors Shopify's merchant lock-in: technically possible to leave, but the friction is enormous once you have live customers.
   127вҶ’
   128вҶ’### 4.3 Trust Graph
   129вҶ’
   130вҶ’Every completed pickup, every QR verification, every review builds a **local trust graph** that is unique to Local-Roots:
   131вҶ’
   132вҶ’- Buyer-farmer trust scores based on real fulfillment history.
   133вҶ’- Pickup location reliability ratings.
   134вҶ’- Seasonal quality patterns that inform future recommendations.
   135вҶ’
   136вҶ’This data asset compounds over time and cannot be replicated by a new entrant.
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’### 4.4 Brand & Community
   139вҶ’
   140вҶ’Local food is inherently emotional and community-driven. Local-Roots has the opportunity to become synonymous with "supporting your local farmer" in the same way that Etsy became synonymous with handmade goods. Brand trust in this category is powerful because:
   141вҶ’
   142вҶ’- Food is personal -- buyers want to trust the source.
   143вҶ’- Farmers want to associate with a platform that genuinely serves their interests (not extract maximum fees).
   144вҶ’- The buyer-side-only fee (zero seller fees) signals farmer-first alignment.
   145вҶ’
   146вҶ’### 4.5 What Makes This "10x Better"
   147вҶ’
   148вҶ’Compared to the status quo (manual CSAs, Square invoices, Facebook groups, spreadsheets):
   149вҶ’
   150вҶ’| Current Approach | Local-Roots |
   151вҶ’|---|---|
   152вҶ’| Manual subscriber tracking | Automated subscription management |
   153вҶ’| Cash/check/Venmo payment collection | Stripe recurring billing, card on file |
   154вҶ’| Text/email pickup coordination | Structured pickup windows with QR verification |
   155вҶ’| No demand generation | Built-in buyer marketplace |
   156вҶ’| No analytics | Subscription and demand analytics |
   157вҶ’| 0% platform fee but 100% of the work | 7% + $0.35 buyer fee, 90% less operational burden |
   158вҶ’
   159вҶ’---
   160вҶ’
   161вҶ’## 5. Fundraising Narrative
   162вҶ’
   163вҶ’### The Pitch Story
   164вҶ’
   165вҶ’> "The $17.5 billion direct-to-consumer farm market is growing at 4.6% annually, but it runs on spreadsheets, cash, and hope. Local-Roots is building the Shopify for local food -- a subscription commerce platform that gives every farmer recurring revenue and every family a trusted local food source. We are starting with the highest-intent wedge (subscription pickup boxes) and expanding into the full farmer commerce stack."
   166вҶ’
   167вҶ’### Comparable Companies & Valuations
   168вҶ’
   169вҶ’| Company | Category | Valuation / Exit | Relevance |
   170вҶ’|---|---|---|---|
   171вҶ’| **Shopify** | Commerce infrastructure | $130B+ public | End-state vision: infrastructure for a seller category |
   172вҶ’| **Faire** | Wholesale marketplace | $12.4B (2022) | B2B marketplace with local density dynamics |
   173вҶ’| **Instacart** | Grocery marketplace | ~$10B (IPO 2023) | Local food logistics + marketplace model |
   174вҶ’| **Etsy** | Artisan marketplace | ~$7B public | Community-driven, trust-based marketplace |
   175вҶ’| **Barn2Door** | Farm e-commerce SaaS | $19.5M raised, ~$3.1M ARR | Direct competitor, SaaS model only |
   176вҶ’| **Toast** | Restaurant commerce | $14B+ public | Vertical SaaS + payments for a food category |
   177вҶ’
   178вҶ’### Milestone Targets
   179вҶ’
   180вҶ’**Pre-Seed (Current)**
   181вҶ’- Raise: $500K-$750K
   182вҶ’- Milestones: 50 active farmers, 1,000 active buyers, 3 metro areas, product-market fit signals (>60% monthly subscription renewal)
   183вҶ’- Valuation target: $5-8M pre-money
   184вҶ’
   185вҶ’**Seed Round (Month 12-15)**
   186вҶ’- Raise: $2-3M
   187вҶ’- Milestones: 300 farmers, 8,000 buyers, 10 metro areas, $300K monthly GMV, 70%+ farmer 6-month retention
   188вҶ’- Valuation target: $15-20M pre-money
   189вҶ’
   190вҶ’**Series A (Month 24-30)**
   191вҶ’- Raise: $8-12M
   192вҶ’- Milestones: 1,500 farmers, 50,000 buyers, 30+ metro areas, $2M+ monthly GMV, clear path to profitability, SaaS tier launched
   193вҶ’- Valuation target: $50-80M pre-money
   194вҶ’
   195вҶ’### Investor Narrative by Stage
   196вҶ’
   197вҶ’**Pre-Seed:** "We have built the product, signed early farmers, and proven the subscription model works in [first metro]. We need capital to hire a farmer success team and expand to 3 markets."
   198вҶ’
   199вҶ’**Seed:** "We have product-market fit. Farmers who join stay (80%+ retention). Buyers renew subscriptions at 70%+ rates. We need capital to build density in 10 metros and launch our SaaS tier."
   200вҶ’
   201вҶ’**Series A:** "We have a repeatable city-launch playbook, blended take rate approaching 8-10%, and a clear path to $50M ARR. We need capital to expand nationally and build platform services."
   202вҶ’
   203вҶ’---
   204вҶ’
   205вҶ’## 6. Risk Analysis: Top 5 Existential Risks
   206вҶ’
   207вҶ’### Risk 1: Cold Start / Chicken-and-Egg Problem
   208вҶ’**Severity:** Critical
   209вҶ’**Description:** Marketplace requires both farmers and buyers simultaneously. Without farmers, buyers leave. Without buyers, farmers leave.
   210вҶ’**Mitigation:**
   211вҶ’- Launch market-by-market with a "supply-first" strategy: onboard 15-20 farmers before launching buyer-side marketing.
   212вҶ’- Offer free onboarding and migration of existing subscriber lists to give farmers immediate value even without new buyer acquisition.
   213вҶ’- Each farmer brings their existing customer base -- this is "bring your own demand" which partially solves the cold start.
   214вҶ’
   215вҶ’### Risk 2: Competitive Response from Incumbents
   216вҶ’**Severity:** High
   217вҶ’**Description:** Barn2Door ($19.5M raised), Local Line, Harvie, or a new Shopify vertical could target this exact space. A well-funded competitor with existing farmer relationships could move fast.
   218вҶ’**Mitigation:**
   219вҶ’- Competitors are SaaS-only (no marketplace/demand generation). Local-Roots' marketplace component is the differentiator.
   220вҶ’- Move fast to lock in density in core markets. Once a farmer's subscribers are on-platform, switching costs are high.
   221вҶ’- Build community and brand that competitors cannot replicate with features alone.
   222вҶ’- The buyer-side fee at 7% + $0.35 (vs. $99-299/mo SaaS fees) is more farmer-friendly for smaller operations.
   223вҶ’
   224вҶ’### Risk 3: Take Rate Ceiling
   225вҶ’**Severity:** Medium
   226вҶ’**Description:** At 7% + $0.35 buyer-side, net contribution per transaction is $1.64 on a $45 box. While break-even is now achievable at realistic scale (~610 farmers), further revenue growth requires diversification.
   227вҶ’**Mitigation:**
   228вҶ’- Revenue diversification roadmap: SaaS tier, promoted listings, financial services, logistics.
   229вҶ’- The 7% + $0.35 fee is the wedge, not the ceiling. As platform value increases, blended take rate should reach 8-12% through additional services.
   230вҶ’- Marketplace models historically expand take rates as they add services (Shopify: subscriptions to payments to capital to fulfillment).
   231вҶ’
   232вҶ’### Risk 4: Seasonality and Geographic Limitations
   233вҶ’**Severity:** Medium-High
   234вҶ’**Description:** Farming is seasonal. Northern markets may see 4-6 months of reduced activity. This creates revenue volatility and buyer churn during off-seasons.
   235вҶ’**Mitigation:**
   236вҶ’- Prioritize year-round production areas first (California, Texas, Florida, Southeast) for initial expansion.
   237вҶ’- Expand product scope beyond produce to include meat, dairy, eggs, preserved goods, and value-added products that have year-round availability.
   238вҶ’- Introduce "winter shares" and partnerships with greenhouse/indoor farms.
   239вҶ’- Seasonal slowdown in one region can be offset by expansion into counter-seasonal regions.
   240вҶ’
   241вҶ’### Risk 5: Regulatory and Food Safety Liability
   242вҶ’**Severity:** Medium
   243вҶ’**Description:** Food safety incidents on the platform could create liability exposure, regulatory scrutiny, or reputational damage. Cottage food laws and direct-sale regulations vary by state.
   244вҶ’**Mitigation:**
   245вҶ’- Platform is a marketplace, not a food handler -- maintain clear legal separation.
   246вҶ’- Require farmers to attest to applicable licenses and food safety certifications.
   247вҶ’- Build in food safety best practices into onboarding (cold chain guidance, labeling requirements).
   248вҶ’- Maintain comprehensive insurance coverage as platform scales.
   249вҶ’- Proactive engagement with state agriculture departments.
   250вҶ’
   251вҶ’### Risk 6: Off-Platform Payment / Disintermediation
   252вҶ’**Severity:** High
   253вҶ’**Description:** Since buyer and farmer meet in person at every pickup, buyers have an obvious opportunity to bypass the platform and pay the farmer directly (cash, Venmo, etc.) to avoid the 7% + $0.35 service fee. This is the classic marketplace disintermediation risk, amplified by the in-person nature of every transaction.
   254вҶ’
   255вҶ’**Why it's partially self-mitigating:**
   256вҶ’- The fee is buyer-side. The farmer gets their full amount regardless of whether payment goes through the platform. The farmer has **zero financial incentive** to encourage off-platform payment.
   257вҶ’- The farmer actively **wants** buyers on-platform because it eliminates admin burden: automatic recurring billing, subscription management, no-show fee protection, pickup verification, and analytics. Going off-platform means going back to spreadsheets and Venmo chasing.
   258вҶ’- $3.50 on a $45 box is below most people's "bother threshold," especially when the platform provides card-on-file convenience, scheduling, reminders, and one-tap pause/cancel.
   259вҶ’
   260вҶ’**Where the risk is highest:**
   261вҶ’- High-value boxes ($80+) where the fee exceeds $6 and feels visible
   262вҶ’- Long-term loyal buyers who already have the farmer's contact info and don't need the platform's discovery or scheduling value
   263вҶ’- Cash-friendly buyer demographics
   264вҶ’
   265вҶ’**Design mitigations:**
   266вҶ’1. **Don't surface farmer contact info to buyers.** Communication goes through the platform. No phone/email on buyer-facing pages.
   267вҶ’2. **Frame the fee as value, not a tax.** Position as "Platform fee includes pickup scheduling, reminders, and payment protection" rather than a bare line item. Consider embedding the fee in the price (farmer sets buyer price, platform takes 7% + $0.35 from the gross) so the buyer never sees a separate fee.
   268вҶ’3. **Lock operational value into the platform.** The 6-digit pickup code verification flow is key -- if the farmer relies on it for fulfillment tracking and no-show management, taking a buyer off-platform creates an operational gap.
   269вҶ’4. **Build retention features that only work on-platform.** Pause/resume, subscription history, pickup reminders, reviews, referral credits. The more the buyer's "local food life" lives on Local-Roots, the less they think about the fee.
   270вҶ’5. **Cap the service fee.** Cap at $5-6 per transaction so high-value boxes don't feel punitive. This costs very little revenue (most boxes are $35-55) but removes worst-case optics.
   271вҶ’
   272вҶ’**Structural mitigation -- Deposit / No-Show Fee Split:**
   273вҶ’
   274вҶ’The most powerful anti-disintermediation mechanism is to embed the platform in the financial structure of the transaction, not just the convenience layer. The proposed model:
   275вҶ’
   276вҶ’- Every subscription requires a **deposit hold** on the buyer's card (e.g., $5-10) that serves as a no-show guarantee.
   277вҶ’- If the buyer completes their pickup, the deposit is released and only the standard box price is charged.
   278вҶ’- If the buyer no-shows, the deposit is **split between the farmer and the platform** (e.g., 70/30 or 60/40).
   279вҶ’- The farmer benefits because they receive partial compensation for wasted produce. The platform benefits because it earns revenue from the enforcement mechanism.
   280вҶ’
   281вҶ’This creates a dynamic where:
   282вҶ’- **The farmer actively wants buyers on-platform** because the platform protects them from no-show losses. Off-platform buyers have no deposit, no accountability, and cost the farmer money when they flake.
   283вҶ’- **The buyer accepts the deposit** because it's refundable on pickup and demonstrates their commitment. It's the same mechanic as hotel reservations or ClassPass credits.
   284вҶ’- **The platform's revenue is partially tied to enforcement**, not just transaction flow. Even if a buyer tried to pay the box price directly to the farmer, the deposit/no-show infrastructure only works through the platform.
   285вҶ’- **It aligns all three parties:** buyers are incentivized to show up, farmers are protected when they don't, and the platform earns its fee by providing the enforcement layer.
   286вҶ’
   287вҶ’This transforms the platform's value proposition from "convenient payment processing" (replaceable) to "financial accountability infrastructure" (structural). A farmer who has experienced no-show protection will never voluntarily take buyers off-platform.
   288вҶ’
   289вҶ’---
   290вҶ’
   291вҶ’## 7. Path to Scale: From Local Pickup to Billion-Dollar Platform
   292вҶ’
   293вҶ’### Phase 1: Win the Wedge (Months 0-18) -- $0-$500K ARR
   294вҶ’
   295вҶ’**Strategy:** Nail the subscription pickup box use case in 3-5 metro areas.
   296вҶ’
   297вҶ’- Focus: Subscription box creation, Stripe billing, pickup management, QR verification.
   298вҶ’- Markets: Start in 2-3 mid-size metros with strong farm-to-table culture (e.g., Portland, Austin, Raleigh-Durham, Madison).
   299вҶ’- Farmer acquisition: Farmers market field sales, agricultural extension partnerships, local food organization referrals.
   300вҶ’- Buyer acquisition: Organic through farmer cross-promotion (each farmer brings 20-50 existing customers).
   301вҶ’
   302вҶ’**Key bet:** Farmers will migrate existing customers onto the platform for the operational simplicity, giving us a "bring your own demand" advantage over traditional cold-start marketplace problems.
   303вҶ’
   304вҶ’### Phase 2: Build Density (Months 18-36) -- $500K-$3M ARR
   305вҶ’
   306вҶ’**Strategy:** Achieve critical mass in 15-25 metro areas. Launch SaaS tier.
   307вҶ’
   308вҶ’- Expand to top 25 farm-direct metros by population and farm density.
   309вҶ’- Launch seller SaaS tier ($29-99/mo) with advanced analytics, CRM, and multi-location management.
   310вҶ’- Build city-launch playbook: repeatable process for entering a new market with 15+ farmers in 60 days.
   311вҶ’- Introduce buyer retention features: favorites, seasonal recommendations, subscription bundling across farmers.
   312вҶ’
   313вҶ’**Key bet:** City-by-city density creates defensible local market positions that national competitors cannot easily replicate.
   314вҶ’
   315вҶ’### Phase 3: Platform Expansion (Months 36-60) -- $3M-$15M ARR
   316вҶ’
   317вҶ’**Strategy:** Expand beyond pickup boxes into the full farmer commerce stack.
   318вҶ’
   319вҶ’- **Product expansion:** Add one-time purchases, market-day sales (non-subscription), wholesale/restaurant channels.
   320вҶ’- **Logistics layer:** Optional delivery coordination for farmers who want to offer home delivery (partner model, not first-party).
   321вҶ’- **Financial services:** Working capital advances for farmers (revenue-based financing against subscription receivables), crop insurance partnerships.
   322вҶ’- **Data products:** Demand forecasting, pricing optimization, seasonal planning tools.
   323вҶ’- **Promoted listings:** Paid placement in buyer search and discovery.
   324вҶ’
   325вҶ’**Key bet:** Farmers who start with subscriptions expand their use of the platform as we add more tools, increasing ARPU and lock-in.
   326вҶ’
   327вҶ’### Phase 4: National Platform (Months 60-84) -- $15M-$80M ARR
   328вҶ’
   329вҶ’**Strategy:** Become the default infrastructure for direct-to-consumer farm sales in North America.
   330вҶ’
   331вҶ’- 100+ metro areas, 15,000+ active farmers, 500,000+ active buyers.
   332вҶ’- Launch Local-Roots Capital (revenue-based financing for farmers).
   333вҶ’- Enterprise tier for farm cooperatives and multi-farm operations.
   334вҶ’- API platform: let third-party developers build on Local-Roots data and infrastructure.
   335вҶ’- International expansion: begin with Canada, explore UK and Australia (similar farming structures).
   336вҶ’- Blended take rate: 10-12% through diversified revenue streams.
   337вҶ’
   338вҶ’### Phase 5: Category Platform (Year 7+) -- $80M+ ARR, Path to $1B
   339вҶ’
   340вҶ’**Strategy:** Expand the platform model beyond farms to adjacent local/artisan commerce.
   341вҶ’
   342вҶ’- **Category expansion:** Local bakeries, artisan food producers, small-batch beverages, specialty goods.
   343вҶ’- **B2B marketplace:** Farmer-to-restaurant, farmer-to-retailer channels.
   344вҶ’- **Consumer brand:** "Powered by Local-Roots" becomes a trust signal for local food, similar to "Sold on Etsy."
   345вҶ’- **Acquisition targets:** Complementary tools (farm management software, cold chain logistics, local delivery networks).
   346вҶ’
   347вҶ’### The $1B Valuation Math
   348вҶ’
   349вҶ’| Scenario | Farmers | Avg Rev/Farmer | ARR | Revenue Multiple | Valuation |
   350вҶ’|---|---|---|---|---|---|
   351вҶ’| Conservative (Year 5) | 10,000 | $4,000 | $40M | 10x | $400M |
   352вҶ’| Base case (Year 7) | 25,000 | $5,500 | $137M | 8x | $1.1B |
   353вҶ’| Upside (Year 7) | 30,000 | $7,000 | $210M | 8x | $1.7B |
   354вҶ’
   355вҶ’The base case assumes:
   356вҶ’- 25,000 active farmers (out of ~130,000 US farms doing direct sales)
   357вҶ’- ~19% market penetration of digitally-active direct-sale farms
   358вҶ’- Blended ARPU of $5,500/farmer/year (subscriptions + SaaS + services)
   359вҶ’- 8x forward revenue multiple (in line with vertical SaaS/marketplace comps)
   360вҶ’
   361вҶ’### Potential Acquirers & Strategic Partners
   362вҶ’
   363вҶ’| Company | Strategic Rationale |
   364вҶ’|---|---|
   365вҶ’| **Shopify** | Expands into perishable/local commerce vertical they cannot serve with current tools |
   366вҶ’| **Square/Block** | Extends farmer seller ecosystem (already strong in farmers markets via Square POS) |
   367вҶ’| **Instacart** | Adds subscription/recurring model and farm-direct supply to their grocery platform |
   368вҶ’| **DoorDash** | Local food infrastructure complements their delivery network |
   369вҶ’| **Amazon (Whole Foods)** | Local sourcing pipeline and community brand they cannot build internally |
   370вҶ’| **Toast** | Extends from restaurant commerce into farm-to-table supply chain |
   371вҶ’
   372вҶ’---
   373вҶ’
   374вҶ’## 8. Strategic Priorities (Next 12 Months)
   375вҶ’
   376вҶ’1. **Ship Stripe integration and launch paid transactions** -- The product must handle real money before anything else matters.
   377вҶ’2. **Onboard first 50 farmers in 2-3 metros** -- Prove supply-side acquisition and retention in concentrated markets.
   378вҶ’3. **Achieve 60%+ monthly subscription renewal rate** -- This is the single most important product-market fit signal.
   379вҶ’4. **Raise pre-seed round ($500-750K)** -- Fund 12-18 months of runway for team of 3-4.
   380вҶ’5. **Build the farmer success playbook** -- Repeatable onboarding process that gets a farmer from signup to first subscriber in under 7 days.
   381вҶ’
   382вҶ’---
   383вҶ’
   384вҶ’## 9. Why Now?
   385вҶ’
   386вҶ’Three macro tailwinds converge to make this the right moment:
   387вҶ’
   388вҶ’1. **Post-COVID consumer behavior shift is permanent.** Direct-to-consumer farm sales grew 35% in 2020 and have not reverted. The farm subscription box market is now valued at $8.2B with 19% annual growth. Consumers discovered local food during COVID and many have not gone back.
   389вҶ’
   390вҶ’2. **Farmers are digitizing, but tools are fragmented.** Barn2Door, Local Line, and Harvie have proven farmers will adopt software, but no one has built the marketplace layer. Farmer e-commerce is where restaurant tech was before Toast: SaaS tools exist, but no integrated commerce platform has emerged.
   391вҶ’
   392вҶ’3. **Subscription commerce infrastructure is mature.** Stripe's subscription billing, identity verification, and Connect platform make it possible for a small team to build enterprise-grade payment infrastructure. Five years ago, this would have required 10x the engineering investment.
   393вҶ’
   394вҶ’The window is open. The market is growing. The tools are ready. The competition is fragmented. The next 18 months determine whether Local-Roots captures this opportunity or someone else does.
   395вҶ’
   396вҶ’---
   397вҶ’
   398вҶ’*This document should be revisited quarterly and updated as market conditions and company metrics evolve.*
   399вҶ’
